walkthrough.title: Sort the Resource Cubes
walkthrough.text: Let's get It's a Wonderful World set up. First, find the five types of resource cubes. White cubes are Materials, black cubes are Energy, green cubes are Science, yellow cubes are Gold, and blue cubes are Exploration. Sort them into five separate piles in the middle of the table where everyone can reach them.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Character Tokens
walkthrough.text: Now find the character tokens. There are two types: Financiers and Generals. Each one is worth one victory point at the end of the game. Place them in two separate supply piles next to the resource cubes. We will explain how you earn these during the rules.

walkthrough.title: Set Out Krystallium Tokens
walkthrough.text: Next, find the Krystallium tokens. These are the red wild resource tokens. Place them in their own supply pile near the other resources. Krystallium can substitute for any resource type when building cards, so they are very valuable.

walkthrough.title: Shuffle the Development Deck
walkthrough.text: Grab the big deck of development cards. These are the project cards you will be drafting and building throughout the game. Give the deck a thorough shuffle and place it face down in the center of the table.

walkthrough.title: Set the Round Tracker
walkthrough.text: Find the round tracker and set it to round one. The game lasts exactly four rounds. Each round you will draft cards, then produce resources in a fixed order to complete your projects.

walkthrough.title: Hand Out Empire Cards
walkthrough.text: Give each player one empire card. For your first game, use the identical basic side, which gives everyone the same starting production. The other side has unique asymmetric powers for advanced play. Your empire card shows what resources you produce at the start of each production step.

walkthrough.title: Deal Starting Hands
walkthrough.text: Deal seven development cards face down to each player from the shuffled deck. These are the cards you will draft in the first round. Go ahead and look at your cards, but keep them hidden from other players. Everyone should have exactly seven cards in hand.

walkthrough.title: Choose a Start Player
walkthrough.text: Pick someone to go first. The starting player does not have a big advantage in this game since everyone drafts simultaneously. The start player just helps coordinate the passing direction. In round one, you will pass cards to the left. Ready? Let's learn the rules.

bullets: Separate Financiers and Generals into 2 supply piles
Each token = 1 VP at end of game

bullets: Place red Krystallium tokens in their own supply pile
Krystallium = wild resource, substitutes for any type

bullets: Shuffle all 150 development cards into one face-down deck
Place centrally where all players can reach

bullets: Pick a random start player to coordinate drafting

All drafting is simultaneous — no major first-player advantage

walkthrough.title: Game Overview
walkthrough.text: It's a Wonderful World is played over four rounds. Each round has two phases: a drafting phase where everyone picks cards, and a production phase where you generate resources to build those cards. The player with the most victory points from completed cards and character tokens at the end of four rounds wins.

walkthrough.title: Drafting Phase: Pick and Pass
walkthrough.text: Let's start with drafting. Everyone looks at their seven cards and picks one to keep. Then you pass the remaining six cards to the next player. You pick one from the new hand, pass five, and keep going until all seven cards have been drafted. This is simultaneous, so everyone drafts at the same time.

walkthrough.title: Construct or Recycle
walkthrough.text: After drafting all seven cards, you make a critical decision for each one. You can either construct it by placing it in your construction area to build over time, or recycle it by discarding it to immediately gain one resource cube of the type shown on the card's recycling bonus. You must choose construct or recycle for every drafted card before production begins.

walkthrough.title: Reading a Development Card
walkthrough.text: Let's look at what a development card shows you. The top area lists the construction cost, which is the combination of resource cubes needed to complete it. The card also shows its victory point value, what it produces once completed, and its recycling bonus in the corner. Some cards also produce character tokens or Krystallium once built.

walkthrough.title: Production Phase: The Fixed Order
walkthrough.text: Now for the heart of the game. Resources are produced in a strict fixed order: Materials first, then Energy, then Science, then Gold, then Exploration. This order never changes and it is printed on your empire card. Understanding this order is the single most important thing in the game.

walkthrough.title: How Production Works
walkthrough.text: During each production step, count up all the matching icons on your empire card and your completed development cards. That is how many cubes of that type you receive. Take those cubes and immediately place them on cards under construction, or leave them aside. You must place or discard all cubes before moving to the next resource type.

walkthrough.title: Completing Cards Mid-Production
walkthrough.text: Here is where it gets exciting. When a card under construction has all its required cubes placed on it, it is completed immediately. Flip it over to your empire and add its production to your engine right away. If that card produces a resource type that has not been produced yet this round, it will contribute during that later step. This means completing a card during Materials production can boost your Energy, Science, Gold, and Exploration production in the same round.

walkthrough.title: Chaining Completions
walkthrough.text: Chains are the most powerful mechanic in the game. If completing one card gives you production that finishes a second card, that second card also completes immediately. And if the second card's production finishes a third card, that completes too. Long chains of completions in a single production step can be devastatingly powerful. Planning these chains is what separates good players from great ones.

walkthrough.title: Supremacy Bonuses
walkthrough.text: During each resource production step, the player who produces the most of that resource earns a supremacy bonus. For Materials and Energy, you earn a General token. For Science, Gold, and Exploration, you earn a Financier token. Each token is worth one victory point. If players tie for the most production, all tied players receive the bonus.

walkthrough.title: Unused Resources and Krystallium
walkthrough.text: At the end of the production phase, any resource cubes that were not placed on cards are lost. However, for every five unused cubes you discard, you receive one Krystallium token. Krystallium is a wild resource that can substitute for any cube type when constructing cards. It never expires between rounds.

walkthrough.title: End of Round
walkthrough.text: After all five resource types have been produced, the round is over. Advance the round tracker. Deal seven new cards to each player and begin the next drafting phase. Any cards still under construction carry over to the next round with their cubes still on them. You do not lose progress on unfinished projects.

walkthrough.title: Scoring and Endgame
walkthrough.text: After four rounds, add up your final score. Count the victory points printed on all your completed development cards. Then add one point for each Financier and General token you have collected. Uncompleted cards score zero points. The player with the highest total wins. If there is a tie, the player with the most character tokens wins.

summary.title: Game Overview
bullets: 4 rounds, each with a Draft phase and a Production phase
Draft development cards → produce resources → complete cards
Most VP from completed cards + character tokens wins

walkthrough.title: Welcome to Your First Round
walkthrough.text: Welcome to your first round of It's a Wonderful World! In this practice tutorial, we will walk through a scripted first round with three example players. Each player will make different choices so you can see how drafting, recycling, and production all work together. Follow along even if you have a different player count.

walkthrough.title: The Starting Position
walkthrough.text: Each player has their empire card in front of them showing their starting production. On the basic side, everyone produces one Material, one Energy, and one Science. That is not much, so the cards you draft and the recycling bonuses you collect will be critical for completing your first projects.

walkthrough.title: First Draft Pick: Player One
walkthrough.text: Player One looks at their seven cards and spots a card that costs just two Materials and one Energy to build. It produces one Science and is worth three victory points. That is cheap and efficient. Player One picks this card and passes the remaining six to the left.

walkthrough.title: First Draft Pick: Player Two
walkthrough.text: Player Two finds a card that costs three Materials but produces two Energy once completed. It is not worth many points, but that Energy production will feed bigger projects later. Player Two picks this production-focused card. Building your engine early is more important than chasing points.

walkthrough.title: First Draft Pick: Player Three
walkthrough.text: Player Three sees a flashy card worth seven victory points, but it costs two of every resource type. That is ten cubes total. Player Three picks it anyway, thinking the points are too good to pass up. We will see how that works out.

walkthrough.title: Completing the Draft
walkthrough.text: Everyone continues drafting. Pick one, pass the rest, pick one, pass the rest. After seven picks, each player has a full hand of seven drafted cards. Now comes the big decision: for each card, do you construct it or recycle it?

walkthrough.title: Construct or Recycle Decisions
walkthrough.text: Player One decides to construct four cards and recycle three. The recycled cards give Player One two Material cubes and one Energy cube, which go straight onto cards under construction. Player Two constructs three cards and recycles four, banking cubes to complete a cheap card right away. Player Three tries to construct six cards and only recycles one.

walkthrough.title: Mistake: Constructing Too Many Cards
walkthrough.text: Player Three just made a classic beginner mistake. They have six cards under construction, but their empire only produces three cubes per round. It will take several rounds to finish even one of those expensive cards. Meanwhile, Players One and Two recycled aggressively and already have cubes on their projects. Do not try to build everything you draft.

walkthrough.title: Materials Production Step
walkthrough.text: Let's start producing. Materials go first. Everyone counts their Material production icons. Each player's empire card produces one Material, so everyone takes one white cube. Player One places it on their cheapest card, which now has two of its three required cubes. Player Two does the same. Player Three stares at six unfinished cards and places one cube on the seven-point dream card.

walkthrough.title: Energy Production Step
walkthrough.text: Next up is Energy. Each empire produces one Energy, so everyone takes one black cube. Player One places it on their cheap card, which now has all three required cubes. That card is complete! Player One flips it into their empire immediately. It produces one Science, which will count during the Science step coming up next.

walkthrough.title: Why Early Completion Matters
walkthrough.text: Notice what just happened. Player One completed a card during Energy production, and that card produces Science. When Science production happens in just a moment, Player One will get an extra Science cube. A card completed early in the production order gives you bonus output for the rest of that round. This is the core engine-building loop of the game.

walkthrough.title: Mistake: Ignoring Production Order
walkthrough.text: Player Three's dream card needs two of every resource, but it will not complete until all five resource types have been placed. That means even if Player Three fills it this round, it will not produce anything until next round. Compare that to Player One's cheap card that completed during Energy and immediately boosted Science production. Always think about when in the production order your card will finish.

walkthrough.title: Science, Gold, and Exploration
walkthrough.text: Let's finish the production phase. During Science production, Player One gets two Science cubes thanks to the newly completed card. Player Two completes their cheap production card and adds two Energy icons to their empire for next round. Player Three manages to place cubes but completes nothing. Gold and Exploration production follow the same pattern.

walkthrough.title: Supremacy Bonus Check
walkthrough.text: After each resource step, we check for supremacy. Player Two produced the most Energy this round because their completed card already had Energy icons. Player Two earns a General token worth one victory point. Ties give the bonus to all tied players, so keep an eye on what your opponents are producing.

walkthrough.title: Mistake: Spreading Resources Too Thin
walkthrough.text: At the end of Round 1, Player Three has six cards under construction and none of them are complete. Their cubes are spread across too many projects, and they have zero completed cards boosting production. This is the worst position to be in. Next round, Player Three needs to focus on finishing one or two cheap cards rather than starting more projects.

walkthrough.title: End of Round Cleanup
walkthrough.text: The round is over. Any cubes that were not placed on cards are lost. If anyone had five or more unplaced cubes, they would convert every five into one Krystallium token. Advance the round tracker to round two and deal seven new cards to each player. Unfinished construction carries over with all cubes intact.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: Congratulations, you have completed your first round of It's a Wonderful World! You have seen how drafting, recycling, and the fixed production order work together. You watched Player Three learn that building too many cards at once spreads you too thin, that ignoring production order wastes potential, and that chasing expensive cards early slows your engine. Focus on cheap, early-completing cards that boost your production for future rounds.

walkthrough.title: What's Next
walkthrough.text:
Before you dive into a full four-round game, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for strategy basics like when to recycle and how to read the production order.

Second, reference the Appendix for a quick guide to resource types, card anatomy, and scoring.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

walkthrough.title: Recycle Aggressively
walkthrough.text: The most common beginner mistake is trying to build every card you draft. In reality, you should recycle about half your cards each round. Recycling gives you an immediate resource cube that can help complete a card under construction right now. A cube in hand today is worth more than a card you might finish in two rounds. When in doubt, recycle.

walkthrough.title: Respect the Production Order
walkthrough.text: The fixed production order is the most important concept in the game. A card that completes during Materials production gives you its bonus output for Energy, Science, Gold, and Exploration that same round. A card that completes during Exploration gives you nothing extra until next round. Always prioritize cards with costs weighted toward early resources like Materials and Energy so they complete as early as possible in the production chain.

walkthrough.title: Build Your Engine in Rounds One and Two
walkthrough.text: Cards completed in round one produce resources for all four rounds. Cards completed in round four only score their victory points. This means production-focused cards are most valuable early, while high-point cards are better late. In rounds one and two, prioritize cards that boost your resource production even if their point value is low. Save the big point cards for rounds three and four when your engine can afford them.

walkthrough.title: Focus on Two or Three Resource Types
walkthrough.text: Trying to produce all five resource types equally will leave you mediocre at everything. Instead, specialize in two or three types and draft cards whose costs match your production strengths. If your engine produces lots of Materials and Energy, draft cards that cost Materials and Energy. This makes your production more efficient and helps you win supremacy bonuses in your focused types.

walkthrough.title: Plan for Chains
walkthrough.text: The most explosive plays in the game come from chain completions. Look for sequences where completing card A gives you the production to finish card B in the same step. For example, if card A produces two Science and card B needs exactly two Science, completing A during any step before Science triggers a chain. Setting up chains takes practice, but even one successful chain per round can launch you ahead of the competition.

walkthrough.title: Watch What Opponents Draft
walkthrough.text: Pay attention to what cards your opponents are taking during the draft. If someone is heavily investing in Energy production, they will likely dominate the Energy supremacy bonus. You can adjust by either competing for the same bonus or pivoting to a resource type nobody else is focused on. Occasionally, hate-drafting a card that perfectly fits an opponent's engine is worth it, especially if you can recycle it for a useful cube.

walkthrough.title: Do Not Hoard Krystallium
walkthrough.text: Krystallium tokens are wild resources, but they come from wasting five cubes. If you are regularly converting unused cubes to Krystallium, it means your engine is leaking. The best players place nearly every cube they produce onto cards under construction. An occasional Krystallium from leftover cubes is fine, but do not plan around generating them.

walkthrough.title: Evaluate Card Efficiency
walkthrough.text: Not all cards are equal. When evaluating a development card, divide its victory points by its total resource cost. A six-point card costing three cubes is twice as efficient as a six-point card costing six cubes. Also factor in the card's production bonus. A card worth two points that produces two Energy every round for three rounds is effectively worth eight points of value when you count the resources it generates.

bullets: Recycle ~half your drafted cards each round
Immediate cube now > card you might finish in 2 rounds
Don't try to build everything you draft

Cards completing early in the order boost later steps this round
Prioritize cards with early-resource costs (Materials, Energy)

summary.title: Build Your Engine in Rounds One and Two
bullets: Round 1 cards produce for all 4 rounds; Round 4 cards only score VP
Early rounds: prioritize production cards over VP cards
Late rounds: pivot to high-VP cards your engine can afford

summary.title: Focus on Two or Three Resource Types

Draft cards whose costs match your production strengths
Focused production → more efficient building + supremacy bonuses

summary.title: Plan for Chains
bullets: Chain: completing card A triggers completion of card B in same step
Look for production → cost matches between cards under construction
Even 1 chain per round creates a huge advantage

summary.title: Watch What Opponents Draft

Compete for their supremacy bonus or pivot to an open type

summary.title: Do Not Hoard Krystallium
bullets: Krystallium comes from wasting 5 cubes — a sign of inefficiency
Best players place nearly every produced cube on cards
Don't plan your engine around generating Krystallium

summary.title: Evaluate Card Efficiency
bullets: VP ÷ total resource cost = efficiency ratio
Factor in production bonus: ongoing resources = hidden VP
A 2 VP card producing 2 Energy for 3 rounds ≈ 8 VP of value

walkthrough.title: The Rush Strategy
walkthrough.text: The rush strategy focuses on completing as many cheap cards as possible in rounds one and two. Draft low-cost cards with one to three cubes total, and recycle everything else. Your goal is to have five or six completed cards by the end of round two, giving you massive production for rounds three and four. The rush player often leads in supremacy bonuses because their production ramps fastest.

walkthrough.title: The Big Card Strategy
walkthrough.text: The opposite approach targets expensive cards worth seven to ten victory points each. You spend rounds one and two building production infrastructure, then use rounds three and four to power through massive cards. The key is patience. Do not draft expensive cards until you have the engine to finish them quickly. A single round-three chain that completes two eight-point cards can swing the entire game.

walkthrough.title: Supremacy Farming
walkthrough.text: Character tokens from supremacy bonuses are often undervalued. Across four rounds and five resource types, there are twenty possible supremacy bonuses. If you dominate two resource types, you can earn eight tokens worth eight victory points without any card costs. To farm supremacy, specialize your production in one or two types and draft cards that produce those types. In a close game, supremacy tokens are often the margin of victory.

walkthrough.title: Reading the Draft
walkthrough.text: Advanced players track not just which cards they want but which cards are circulating. If you see strong Energy cards passing through your hands, it likely means nobody at the table is building an Energy engine. That is your signal to pivot into Energy. Conversely, if you never see Science cards, someone is snapping them all up. Do not fight over a contested resource type when an uncontested one is available.

walkthrough.title: Recycling as a Weapon
walkthrough.text: Expert players use recycling strategically, not just for cubes. Recycling a card that would perfectly chain into an opponent's engine is a form of denial. You get a useful cube, and they lose a key piece. When the draft hands are getting small, look at what your neighbor needs and consider recycling it rather than constructing something marginal for yourself.

walkthrough.title: Chain Architecture
walkthrough.text: The best players plan their chains two or three steps deep. They set up a sequence where completing card A during Materials production creates enough cubes to finish card B during Energy, which in turn completes card C during Science. Building these multi-step chains requires careful cost analysis. Line up cards whose costs match the production of cards earlier in the chain, and pre-load cubes using recycled resources during the draft phase.

walkthrough.title: Late-Game Pivot
walkthrough.text: In rounds three and four, your priorities shift completely. Production value no longer matters because there are not enough rounds left to recoup the investment. Instead, draft the highest-VP cards you can finish this round. Recycle even more aggressively to pre-load cubes. Your engine should be strong enough by now to complete expensive cards in a single production phase. Every cube that does not convert to victory points is wasted.

walkthrough.title: Empire Card Asymmetry
walkthrough.text: Once you move past the basic empire card, each player starts with different production and a unique recycling bonus. This dramatically changes the draft. If your empire card produces two Materials, cards costing Materials become cheaper for you than for anyone else. Lean into your empire card's strengths in round one to build a faster engine. The asymmetric powers create different optimal strategies for each player, so do not copy what worked last game if you have a different empire card.

summary.title: The Rush Strategy
bullets: Draft only cheap cards (1-3 cubes), recycle everything else
Target 5-6 completed cards by end of Round 2
Massive production ramp → dominate Rounds 3-4
Often leads in supremacy bonuses from fast production

summary.title: The Big Card Strategy
bullets: Rounds 1-2: build production infrastructure
Rounds 3-4: power through 7-10 VP cards
Patience is key — don't draft expensive cards too early
One chain completing two 8-point cards = game-winning swing

summary.title: Supremacy Farming
bullets: 20 possible supremacy bonuses across 4 rounds × 5 resource types
Dominating 2 types = ~8 tokens = 8 VP for free
Specialize production in 1-2 types to farm consistently
Often the margin of victory in close games

summary.title: Reading the Draft
bullets: Track which resource cards circulate vs. get snapped up
Cards passing through = uncontested resource type → pivot in
Cards disappearing = contested type → avoid or compete
Adapt strategy based on what the draft tells you

summary.title: Recycling as a Weapon
bullets: Recycle cards that fit opponents' engines = denial + cube
Especially effective in small draft hands (picks 5-7)
A useful cube for you + blocking an opponent = double value
Don't build marginal cards when recycling helps more

summary.title: Chain Architecture
bullets: Plan chains 2-3 steps deep: A → B → C across production steps
Match card costs to production of earlier-completing cards
Pre-load cubes via recycling during the draft phase
Multi-step chains are the highest-skill play in the game

summary.title: Late-Game Pivot
bullets: Rounds 3-4: production value irrelevant, only VP matters
Draft highest-VP cards you can finish this round
Recycle even more aggressively to pre-load cubes
Every unused cube = wasted potential VP

summary.title: Empire Card Asymmetry
bullets: Advanced empire cards: unique starting production + recycling bonus
Lean into your empire's strengths in Round 1
Cards matching your empire's production become cheaper for you
Different empire = different optimal strategy each game

entries.definition: The first resource produced each round. White cubes. Produced first in the fixed order, making cards that cost Materials the earliest to complete in a production phase.
The second resource produced each round. Black cubes. Produced after Materials, making it an early-chain resource that helps trigger mid-production completions.
The third resource produced each round. Green cubes. The middle resource in the production chain, often used for technology and research-themed development cards.
The fourth resource produced each round. Yellow cubes. A late-chain resource, meaning cards requiring Gold are harder to complete early in a production phase.
The fifth and final resource produced each round. Blue cubes. Cards completed during Exploration production gain no additional output that round since production is already finished.

A wild resource token that can substitute for any cube type when constructing cards. Earned by converting every five unused cubes at the end of a production phase, or produced directly by certain completed development cards. Krystallium persists between rounds.

The main cards drafted and built throughout the game. Each card shows a construction cost, victory point value, production bonus once completed, and a recycling bonus if discarded. There are 150 development cards in the base game.

Each player's personal board showing starting production values. The basic side is identical for all players. The advanced side has unique asymmetric powers and different starting production for each player.

The combination of resource cubes needed to complete a development card, shown along the top of the card. Cubes are placed on the card during production until all required resources are present, at which point the card completes immediately.
The point value printed on a completed development card. Only completed cards score their VP at game end. Uncompleted cards are worth zero points. Typical values range from one to ten or more.
The resource icons a completed development card adds to your empire. These icons count during every future production phase, compounding your output each round the card is in play.
The resource type you receive when you discard a drafted card instead of constructing it. Each card gives exactly one cube of the indicated type. Shown in the corner of the card.

A character token earned via supremacy bonuses during Science, Gold, or Exploration production. Each Financier is worth one victory point at end of game. Some development cards also interact with Financier tokens.
A character token earned via supremacy bonuses during Materials or Energy production. Each General is worth one victory point at end of game. Some development cards also interact with General tokens.

During each resource production step, the player who produces the most of that resource earns a character token. Materials and Energy award Generals. Science, Gold, and Exploration award Financiers. If players tie, all tied players receive the token.

The first phase of each round. Players receive seven development cards, pick one to keep, and pass the rest. This repeats until all seven are drafted. After drafting, each card is assigned to construction or recycling.

The second phase of each round. Resources are produced in fixed order: Materials, Energy, Science, Gold, Exploration. Players receive cubes based on their empire card and completed development cards, then place cubes on cards under construction.

The space in front of each player where development cards being built are placed face-up. Cubes are placed on these cards during production. Cards remain here across rounds until completed or abandoned.

When completing one card during production immediately provides enough resources to complete a second card in the same production step. Chains can cascade multiple times and are the most powerful mechanic in the game.

Discarding a drafted development card to immediately gain one resource cube of the type shown on the card's recycling bonus. Done during the drafting phase before production begins. A key strategic tool for accelerating construction.

The unchanging sequence in which resources are produced each round: Materials, Energy, Science, Gold, Exploration. Printed on each empire card. Cards that complete early in this order give bonus production for the remaining steps that round.
Marks which of the four rounds is currently being played. The game ends after the fourth round's production phase is complete.
Collective term for Financier and General tokens. Each is worth one victory point at game end. Earned through supremacy bonuses and sometimes through development card abilities.
After four rounds, total VP from completed development cards plus one VP per character token. Uncompleted cards score zero. Tiebreaker: most character tokens, then most completed cards.
